blocks.content: The reception disappeared behind them in the dark.

One turn of the road and then the torchlight was gone, the fire was gone, the beach was gone. The ordinary world came back — streetlamps, pavement, the particular quiet of a highway at the edge of a night that didn't know what had happened in it.

Cassandra watched the darkness through her window. The radio was low, something instrumental they'd agreed on without discussing it. Ethan drove the way he did everything: attentively, without performance.
Five hours to the mountains. Three tonight, two tomorrow. The plan had been simple when they'd made it. Now that it was happening, nothing about it felt simple.
Not bad.
Just — real in a way plans aren't, until they are.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: We're married.
She said it to the window, mostly. Testing it again.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yeah.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That's — I keep saying it to myself and it keeps feeling like I'm describing someone else.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Give it a few miles.
She looked at him.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Did it feel real to you? When it was happening?
He thought about it. Not the performative pause — the real one.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: When you walked in.
She waited.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Before that I was just managing the morning. But when I saw you in the doorway — that was when it was real.
She held that. Outside, the city thinned and the road opened up — longer stretches between lights, more sky. The mountains were still five hours away but the direction was set.
She reached across the console and put her hand in his.
He turned his hand over without looking.
The radio played something without words.
The road kept going.
